section.title: The Fall of Jericho
section.sectionRationale: The first city of the conquest falls, and it falls in the strangest possible way. God's battle plan is not a siege but a procession: march around the walls behind the ark, blow the horns, keep silent, and on the seventh day shout. The wall collapses, the city is devoted to the LORD, and only Rahab and her household are spared. It is one movement, the fall of Jericho, which teaches from the outset that the land is the LORD's gift, taken by faith and obedience, not by military might.
section.thesis: God gives Jericho to Israel before a blow is struck, so the bizarre, unwarlike strategy strips away every ground for boasting and makes the victory unmistakably His; the walls fall by faith, the guilty city comes under judgment, and the believing outsider is saved.
section.frameNote: Four beats: God's promise and the strange battle plan, verses 1 to 5; the six days of silent marching, verses 6 to 14; the seventh day, the shout, and the fall of the wall, verses 15 to 21; and Rahab spared while the city is burned, with Joshua's curse, verses 22 to 27.

units.label: The promise and the strange battle plan
units.note: I have delivered Jericho into your hand

verses.text: Now Jericho was tightly shut up because of the Israelites. No one went out and no one came in.
And the LORD said to Joshua, “Behold, I have delivered Jericho into your hand, along with its king and its mighty men of valor.
March around the city with all the men of war, circling the city one time. Do this for six days.
Have seven priests carry seven rams’ horns in front of the ark. Then on the seventh day, march around the city seven times, while the priests blow the horns.

And when there is a long blast of the ram’s horn and you hear its sound, have all the people give a mighty shout. Then the wall of the city will collapse and all your people will charge straight into the city.”

units.label: Six days of silent marching
units.note: do not let one word come out of your mouth until I tell you

verses.text: So Joshua son of Nun summoned the priests and said, “Take up the ark of the covenant and have seven priests carry seven rams’ horns in front of the ark of the LORD.”
And he told the people, “Advance and march around the city, with the armed troops going ahead of the ark of the LORD.”
After Joshua had spoken to the people, seven priests carrying seven rams’ horns before the LORD advanced and blew the horns, and the ark of the covenant of the LORD followed them.
While the horns continued to sound, the armed troops marched ahead of the priests who blew the horns, and the rear guard followed the ark.
But Joshua had commanded the people: “Do not give a battle cry or let your voice be heard; do not let one word come out of your mouth until the day I tell you to shout. Then you are to shout!”
So he had the ark of the LORD carried around the city, circling it once. And the people returned to the camp and spent the night there.
Joshua got up early the next morning, and the priests took the ark of the LORD.

And the seven priests carrying seven rams’ horns kept marching ahead of the ark of the LORD and blowing the horns. The armed troops went in front of them and the rear guard followed the ark of the LORD, while the horns kept sounding.
So on the second day they marched around the city once and returned to the camp. They did this for six days.

units.label: The seventh day and the fall of the wall
units.note: shout, for the LORD has given you the city

verses.text: Then on the seventh day, they got up at dawn and marched around the city seven times in the same manner. That was the only day they circled the city seven times.
After the seventh time around, the priests blew the horns, and Joshua commanded the people, “Shout! For the LORD has given you the city!
Now the city and everything in it must be {{cherem|devoted to the LORD for destruction}}. Only Rahab the prostitute and all those with her in her house will live, because she hid the spies we sent.

But keep away from the things devoted to destruction, lest you yourself be set apart for destruction. If you take any of these, you will set apart the camp of Israel for destruction and bring disaster upon it.
For all the silver and gold and all the articles of bronze and iron are {{qodesh|holy}} to the LORD; they must go into His treasury.”

So when the rams’ horns sounded, the people shouted. When they heard the blast of the horn, the people gave a great {{teruah|shout}}, and the wall collapsed. Then all the people charged straight into the city and captured it.
With the edge of the sword they devoted to destruction everything in the city, man and woman, young and old, oxen, sheep, and donkeys.

units.label: Rahab spared and the city burned
units.note: she has lived among the Israelites to this day

verses.text: Meanwhile, Joshua told the two men who had spied out the land, “Go into the house of the prostitute and bring out the woman and all who are with her, just as you promised her.”
So the young spies went in and brought out Rahab, her father and mother and brothers, and all who belonged to her. They brought out her whole family and settled them outside the camp of Israel.
Then the Israelites burned up the city and everything in it. However, they put the silver and gold and articles of bronze and iron into the treasury of the LORD’s house.

And Joshua spared Rahab the prostitute, with her father’s household and all who belonged to her, because she hid the men Joshua had sent to spy out Jericho. So she has lived among the Israelites to this day.

At that time Joshua invoked this solemn oath: “Cursed before the LORD is the man who rises up and rebuilds this city, Jericho; at the cost of his firstborn he will lay its foundations; at the cost of his youngest he will set up its gates.”
So the LORD was with Joshua, and his fame spread throughout the land.

cherem.gloss: That which is devoted to the LORD irrevocably, set apart either for destruction or for His treasury. Jericho, the firstfruits of the land, is placed wholly under the cherem: its people fall under judgment, and its silver and gold go into the LORD's house. As firstfruits it belongs entirely to God; to seize any of it for oneself, as Achan will, is sacrilege that brings the ban upon the thief.

qodesh.gloss: Holiness, that which is set apart to God. The metals of Jericho are declared holy to the LORD and must enter His treasury, not private hands. Being holy, they are removed from ordinary use and reserved for God; the same holiness that consecrates makes it deadly to treat what is God's as common, tying this chapter to the disaster of chapter 7.

teruah.gloss: A loud shout, a blast, a war cry or cry of acclamation. On the seventh day the silent people finally give the great teruah, and the wall falls. The shout is not what topples the stone; it is the appointed act of faith and worship, a victory cry raised at God's word before the victory is seen, so that the fall is plainly God's doing in response to obedient faith.

basic.context: Jericho is sealed tight, but God tells Joshua the city is already given into his hand. Then comes the plan, and it is nothing like a siege. For six days the army is to march around the city once each day behind the ark, with priests blowing rams' horns, and the people utterly silent. On the seventh day they march around seven times, and at Joshua's word they shout, and the wall collapses. The city is devoted to the LORD, destroyed in judgment, its metals given to God's treasury, while Rahab and her family, who believed, are rescued. Joshua pronounces a curse on anyone who rebuilds it. The question at the heart of it all is: how is the promised land actually won?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and the New Testament reads it through faith: by faith the walls of Jericho fell down, Hebrews 11:30. The strange strategy is the point, designed so no one could credit human might. The chapter also raises the hard question of the herem, the devotion of the city to destruction, which must be handled honestly as God's judgment on entrenched Canaanite sin, with mercy always open, as Rahab shows. We do not derive rules for warfare from this unique, God-commanded event.
basic.originalLanguages: The city is placed under the cherem, devoted wholly to the LORD, its people to destruction and its metals as things qodesh, holy, to God's treasury. And the wall falls only after the people raise the teruah, the great shout, an act of obedient faith at God's word.
basic.typology: The walls falling by faith, apart from human strength, picture the victories God gives His people by faith rather than by their own power. Rahab spared in the midst of judgment pictures the salvation of believers, and the marked house recalls the Passover. Jericho as firstfruits devoted to God points to the firstfruits belonging wholly to the LORD.
basic.christ: Hebrews holds up Jericho as a triumph of faith, and it points to the victory won not by our strength but by Christ. As judgment fell on the guilty city while the believing outsider was saved, so at the cross judgment and mercy meet, and all who take refuge in Christ, like Rahab, are rescued from the wrath to come. Rahab herself enters the line of Jesus.
basic.god: God is the giver of victory: the city is delivered before a blow is struck. He is holy and just, bringing long-delayed judgment on Canaan's sin, and merciful, sparing all who believe. He is worthy of exclusive devotion, so that the firstfruits of the land belong wholly to Him. And He is faithful, being with Joshua just as He promised.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 11 credits the fall of Jericho to faith. Genesis 15 explains that God waited for the sin of the Amorites to reach its full measure. 2 Corinthians 10 says our weapons are not of the flesh. And 1 Kings 16 records the fulfillment of Joshua's curse when Jericho was rebuilt.
basic.oneStory: The first city of the promised land falls by faith, not by force, showing that the inheritance is God's gift. Judgment falls on the guilty city while believing Rahab is saved, a pattern of the gospel. It is a decisive step in God giving Israel the land, and it points to the greater victory and the greater rescue won in Christ.

context.literary: Chapter 6 is carefully patterned around the number seven, seven priests, seven horns, seven days, seven circuits on the seventh day, marking the conquest of Jericho as a sacred, God-ordered event rather than a normal battle. The ark at the center of the procession keeps God's presence in view, echoing chapters 3 and 4. The enforced silence heightens the tension until it breaks in the shout. The chapter is framed by God's opening promise, I have delivered Jericho, and the closing note that the LORD was with Joshua, bracketing the victory as entirely God's work.
context.historical: Jericho was an ancient walled city guarding the entrance to the central hill country from the Jordan valley, the first major obstacle in the land. A city tightly shut up describes a settlement under blockade, its gates barred. Rams' horns, the shofar, were used for signals and for sacred assemblies. The herem, devoting a city entirely to a deity by destruction, was known in the ancient Near East, but here it is uniquely tied to the LORD's righteous judgment on Canaanite wickedness, Deuteronomy 9:4-5, and to His holiness. Joshua's curse on rebuilding Jericho was fulfilled centuries later under Hiel of Bethel, 1 Kings 16:34.
context.canonical: The fall of Jericho becomes a byword for victory by faith, celebrated in Hebrews 11:30. It stands as the firstfruits of the conquest, wholly devoted to God, which frames the tragedy of Achan in chapter 7, who seizes what is holy. Rahab's rescue, set within the judgment, joins her to the New Testament as an example of saving faith and places her in the genealogy of the Messiah. The chapter's presentation of the herem is part of the wider biblical account of God's judgment on sin, which reaches its climax and resolution at the cross.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To narrate the fall of the first city as an act of God given to faith, to show through the strange strategy that the land is the LORD's gift and not Israel's achievement, to display God's holiness in the devoting of the city and His mercy in the rescue of Rahab, and to confirm the LORD's presence with Joshua.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a singular, God-commanded event in redemptive history; it does not prescribe holy war, city-marching, or the herem for any later people, and the church's warfare is expressly spiritual, not by the sword, 2 Corinthians 10:3-4. What endures is the theology it teaches: victory belongs to the LORD and comes by faith, God's judgment on entrenched sin is real and just, and His mercy genuinely saves all who believe.

debates.question: How can the devoting of Jericho to destruction, the herem, be squared with the goodness of God?

views.label: Righteous judgment on ripened wickedness, with mercy open to faith
views.case: Scripture frames the conquest as God's just judgment on centuries of Canaanite evil that He patiently delayed until it was complete, Genesis 15:16 and Deuteronomy 9:4-5; it is judgment, not ethnic cleansing, since Rahab and her household are saved the moment they believe, and Israel itself is warned it will face the same judgment if it copies Canaan's sins, showing the standard is sin, not race.

views.label: A unique, bounded episode that dramatizes final judgment
views.case: The herem was a limited command tied to a specific land and moment in redemptive history, not a template for war; it functions as a vivid, earthly picture of the reality of divine judgment on unrepentant sin, pointing forward to the last judgment, while the salvation of Rahab in the same event holds out the mercy available to all who turn to God.

debates.bearing: Held together honestly, both affirm that God's judgment on sin is real and just and that His mercy is genuinely open, which is precisely why the guilty city falls while the believing prostitute is saved; the cross is where that same judgment and mercy finally meet.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the meaning of Jericho's fall. The city is placed under the cherem, devoted irrevocably to the LORD: as the firstfruits of the whole land it belongs entirely to God, so its people come under His judgment and its precious metals are set apart to Him. Those metals are declared qodesh, holy to the LORD, reserved for His treasury and removed from all common use; the same holiness that consecrates them makes it deadly to grasp them, which is exactly the sin that will destroy Achan and part of Israel in the next chapter. And the wall itself falls only after the long-silent people raise the teruah, the great shout. The shout does not topple the stone by its volume; it is the appointed act of obedient faith and worship, a victory cry lifted at God's word before a single stone has moved, so that when the wall collapses there is no doubt whose doing it is. Devoted, holy, shout: the vocabulary makes plain that Jericho belongs to God, falls to God, and is won by faith in God alone.

typology.type: The walls falling by faith apart from human strength
typology.fulfillment: The victories God gives His people by faith rather than by the arm of the flesh, Hebrews 11:30 and 2 Corinthians 10:3-5.

typology.type: Rahab saved in the midst of the city's judgment
typology.fulfillment: Believers rescued from the wrath to come, and Rahab herself in the line of Christ, Matthew 1:5 and 1 Thessalonians 1:10.

typology.type: Jericho as firstfruits devoted wholly to the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The firstfruits belonging entirely to God, fulfilled in Christ the firstfruits and in a people consecrated to God, 1 Corinthians 15:20 and James 1:18.

study.christ: The letter to the Hebrews sets Jericho among the great acts of faith: by faith the walls of Jericho fell down after the people had marched around them for seven days, Hebrews 11:30. The victory came not by Israel's strength but by trusting God's word, and it points to the greater victory that is never won by the arm of the flesh but received by faith in Christ, who has overcome the world. Deeper still, the chapter sets judgment and mercy side by side: the guilty city falls under the ban, while Rahab, who believed, is drawn out safe with her whole household, her house marked, her life spared. That is the shape of the gospel. At the cross the judgment we deserved fell, and all who take refuge in Christ, like Rahab the outsider, are rescued from the wrath to come and brought into the people of God, so that Rahab herself stands in the family line of Jesus, Matthew 1:5.
study.god: The God of Jericho gives, judges, and saves. He gives the victory before the battle begins, I have delivered Jericho into your hand, and He designs the strange strategy precisely so that no one can boast; the walls fall to His power alone, received by faith. He is holy and just, bringing upon Canaan a judgment He had patiently delayed for generations until its sin was full, and warning Israel that the same holiness will not spare them if they grasp what is His. He is merciful, keeping faith with a Canaanite prostitute and rescuing all who believe with her. And He is faithful to His word: the LORD was with Joshua, just as He had promised at the start. This is a God worthy of exclusive devotion, whose firstfruits belong wholly to Him, and whose judgments and mercy are equally real.

crossReferences.note: By faith the walls of Jericho fell, and by faith Rahab was not destroyed with the disobedient; the chapter's twin themes named as faith.
God waited until the iniquity of the Amorites was complete, showing the conquest as long-delayed, righteous judgment.
Israel receives the land because of the wickedness of the nations and God's promise, not because of Israel's own righteousness.
The believer's weapons are not of the flesh but have divine power; the church's warfare is spiritual, not like Jericho's.
Joshua's curse on rebuilding Jericho is fulfilled in the days of Hiel of Bethel, at the cost of his sons.

study.oneStory: The first city of the promised land falls, and it falls by faith rather than force, so that the whole conquest is stamped from the outset as God's gift received by trusting obedience. Judgment falls on the guilty city while believing Rahab and her household are saved, the gospel pattern in miniature: wrath and mercy meeting in one event. It is a decisive step in God giving Israel the inheritance He swore to Abraham, and it points forward to the greater victory won by Christ and the greater rescue of all who, like Rahab, take refuge in Him.

quiz.q: What was God's battle plan for taking Jericho?

options.text: To march around the city behind the ark for seven days and shout on the seventh, so the wall would fall by God's power.
options.note: Verses 3 to 5 give the strange, unwarlike plan that made the victory unmistakably God's.

options.text: To lay a long siege until the city ran out of food.
options.note: The city was shut up, but God's plan was not a siege; it was the marching, the horns, and the shout.

options.text: To build ramps and storm the walls with ladders.
options.note: There was no assault on the walls; God brought them down Himself after the march and shout.

options.text: To negotiate the city's surrender through Rahab.
options.note: There was no negotiated surrender; the wall fell and the city was taken, only Rahab's household spared.

quiz.q: When did the wall of Jericho collapse?

options.text: After the seven days of marching, when the people gave a great shout at Joshua's command.
options.note: Verse 20 records the wall falling after the appointed shout of faith on the seventh day.

options.text: On the first day, as soon as the priests blew the horns.
options.note: The horns sounded daily, but the wall fell only on the seventh day after the shout, not the first.

options.text: When Joshua's soldiers battered it with rams.
options.note: No battering ram was used; the wall collapsed by God's power after the shout.

options.text: During an earthquake unrelated to Israel's actions.
options.note: The text ties the fall directly to God's promise and the obedient shout, not to a chance event.

quiz.q: What happened to the silver, gold, and metal articles of Jericho?

options.text: They were declared holy to the LORD and put into His treasury.
options.note: Verses 19 and 24 set the metals apart as holy, belonging to God, not to individuals.

options.text: They were divided as plunder among the soldiers.
options.note: Ordinary plunder came in later battles, but Jericho's firstfruits were wholly devoted to God, not shared out.

options.text: They were left in the ruins untouched.
options.note: They were not left behind; they were gathered into the LORD's treasury as holy things.

options.text: They were given to Rahab as a reward.
options.note: Rahab's household was spared and settled, but the metals went into the LORD's treasury, not to her.

quiz.q: Who was spared when Jericho fell, and why?

options.text: Rahab and her household, because she had believed and hidden the spies.
options.note: Verses 22 to 25 record her rescue in fulfillment of the oath, grounded in her faith.

options.text: The king of Jericho, who surrendered at the last moment.
options.note: The king did not surrender or survive; it was Rahab and her family who were spared.

options.text: All the women and children of the city.
options.note: The city came under the ban; only Rahab's household was spared, not all the women and children.

options.text: No one; the entire city was destroyed.
options.note: Rahab and all with her in her house were saved, so not everyone perished.

quiz.q: What does the fall of Jericho teach about how the land was won?

options.text: It was the LORD's gift, taken by faith and obedience, not by Israel's military might.
options.note: The strange strategy and the pre-given promise make the victory God's, received by faith, Hebrews 11:30.

options.text: It was won by superior Israelite tactics and weapons.
options.note: The whole point of the strategy was to exclude human might; the victory was God's, received by faith.

options.text: It proved that God helps only the strongest armies.
options.note: The opposite: God brought the wall down by His power, not because Israel was the strongest.

options.text: It showed that the outcome was really just luck.
options.note: The text credits the LORD, who promised the city and gave it; nothing here is chance.

questions: God had Israel win by marching and shouting rather than by fighting, so no one could boast. Where are you tempted to trust your own strength or strategy instead of trusting God's way?
The people had to stay silent and obedient for six long days before the victory came. How do you handle the waiting when God calls you to keep trusting before you see any results?
Judgment fell on the guilty city while believing Rahab was rescued. How does the fall of Jericho help you hold together both the seriousness of God's judgment and the wideness of His mercy?
Jericho's firstfruits belonged wholly to God, and grasping them would be sacrilege. Is there anything that belongs to God that you are tempted to treat as your own?
Hebrews says the walls fell by faith. Where do you need to raise a shout of faith at God's word, trusting the victory to Him before you can see it?

translationNotes.note: Devoted to the LORD for destruction renders a form of the Hebrew cherem; the BSB footnote explains it as giving persons or things over to the LORD, by destroying or offering them, also in verses 18 and 21.
The wall will collapse renders a phrase the BSB footnote also gives as fall under itself or fall flat; the same expression recurs in verse 20.
The BSB sets off the list man and woman, young and old with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.
Verse 26 is poetic in the BSB; the poem lines have been joined in order as running text. The BSB footnote points to 1 Kings 16:34, where the curse is fulfilled.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 6:1-27. Prose narrative with a poetic oath in verse 26, joined in order as running text. The em dashes in verse 21 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
